title: Nth Fibonacci number
description: Find the Nth Fibonacci number using recursion

examples.explanation: fib(4) calls fib(3) + fib(2). fib(3) calls fib(2) + fib(1). fib(2) calls fib(1) + fib(0). fib(1) returns 1. fib(0) returns 0. So fib(2) returns 1+0=1. fib(1) returns 1. So fib(3) returns 1+1=2. fib(2) calls fib(1) + fib(0) again, returning 1+0=1. So fib(4) returns 2+1=3.

fib(5) calls fib(4) + fib(3). fib(4) calls fib(3) + fib(2). fib(3) calls fib(2) + fib(1). fib(2) calls fib(1) + fib(0). fib(1)=1, fib(0)=0, so fib(2)=1. fib(1)=1, so fib(3)=1+1=2. fib(2) calls fib(1)+fib(0) again, returning 1. So fib(4)=2+1=3. fib(3) calls fib(2)+fib(1) again, fib(2)=1, fib(1)=1, so fib(3)=2. So fib(5)=3+2=5.

approaches.name: Naive Recursion O(2^n)
approaches.summary: Directly implement the Fibonacci definition using recursion without any optimization, leading to exponential time due to repeated calculations of the same subproblems. Use only for educational purposes to illustrate recursion basics, as it is inefficient for practical use.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Compute Nth Fibonacci Number Using Naive Recursion
ladders.desc: Implement the complete naive recursive solution to find the Nth Fibonacci number without any optimization.
ladders.explanation: The naive recursion approach directly implements the mathematical definition of Fibonacci numbers using recursion. The intuition is that each Fibonacci number is the sum of the two preceding ones, so we can recursively break down the problem into smaller subproblems. The algorithm defines a base case for n=0 and n=1, where F(0)=0 and F(1)=1. For n>1, it returns F(n-1) + F(n-2). This leads to exponential time complexity O(2^n) because each call generates two more recursive calls, causing massive recomputation of the same subproblems. The space complexity is O(n) due to the recursion stack depth. This approach is purely educational to illustrate recursion basics and the importance of optimization. Connection to L1: This full solution uses the recursive function as its key subroutine. Common mistakes include forgetting base cases, incorrect recursion termination, and not handling negative inputs. Real code pattern: def fib(n): if n <= 1: return n; return fib(n-1) + fib(n-2).

examples.trace: fib(4) calls fib(3) + fib(2). fib(3) calls fib(2) + fib(1). fib(2) calls fib(1) + fib(0). fib(1) returns 1. fib(0) returns 0. So fib(2) returns 1+0=1. fib(1) returns 1. So fib(3) returns 1+1=2. fib(2) calls fib(1) + fib(0) again, returning 1+0=1. So fib(4) returns 2+1=3.

fib(5) calls fib(4) + fib(3). fib(4) calls fib(3) + fib(2). fib(3) calls fib(2) + fib(1). fib(2) calls fib(1) + fib(0). fib(1)=1, fib(0)=0, so fib(2)=1. fib(1)=1, so fib(3)=1+1=2. fib(2) calls fib(1)+fib(0) again, returning 1. So fib(4)=2+1=3. fib(3) calls fib(2)+fib(1) again, fib(2)=1, fib(1)=1, so fib(3)=2. So fib(5)=3+2=5.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Fibonacci Function
ladders.desc: Implement the recursive function that computes Fibonacci numbers by summing two recursive calls.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core recursive function that defines the Fibonacci sequence. The intuition is to model the mathematical recurrence relation F(n) = F(n-1) + F(n-2) directly in code. The algorithm checks if n is 0 or 1 (base cases) and returns n. Otherwise, it makes two recursive calls: one for n-1 and one for n-2, then returns their sum. Time complexity for this function alone is O(2^n) because each call branches into two, leading to exponential growth in calls. Space complexity is O(n) for the recursion stack. Connection to L0: This function is the entire solution when called with input n. Connection to L2: This implements the core logic of breaking the problem into smaller subproblems. Common mistakes include infinite recursion from missing base cases, off-by-one errors in base conditions, and not handling edge cases like negative n. Real code pattern: def fib_recursive(n): if n <= 1: return n; return fib_recursive(n-1) + fib_recursive(n-2).

examples.trace: fib_recursive(3) checks n>1, so calls fib_recursive(2) + fib_recursive(1). fib_recursive(2) calls fib_recursive(1) + fib_recursive(0). fib_recursive(1) returns 1. fib_recursive(0) returns 0. So fib_recursive(2) returns 1+0=1. fib_recursive(1) returns 1. So fib_recursive(3) returns 1+1=2.
fib_recursive(2) checks n>1, so calls fib_recursive(1) + fib_recursive(0). fib_recursive(1) returns 1. fib_recursive(0) returns 0. So fib_recursive(2) returns 1+0=1.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Recursive Decomposition into Subproblems
ladders.desc: Understand how to break the Fibonacci problem into two smaller recursive subproblems.
ladders.explanation: The core logic is the recursive decomposition strategy: to compute F(n), we need F(n-1) and F(n-2). The intuition is that a problem can be solved by solving smaller instances of the same problem. This is a classic example of divide-and-conquer without the combine step being trivial. The algorithm concept is: if n is base case (0 or 1), return n; otherwise, decompose into problems of size n-1 and n-2, solve them recursively, and combine by addition. Time complexity arises because each decomposition creates two new subproblems, leading to exponential growth. Space complexity is linear in recursion depth. Connection to L1: This logic is implemented in the recursive function. Connection to L3: This relies on understanding function calls and return values. Common mistakes include not properly defining base cases, which causes infinite recursion, or incorrectly combining results. Real code pattern: Conceptually, solve(n) = if base_case: return value; else: return solve(n-1) + solve(n-2).

examples.trace: To find F(4), we decompose it into F(3) and F(2). Then F(3) decomposes into F(2) and F(1). F(2) decomposes into F(1) and F(0). F(1) and F(0) are base cases returning 1 and 0. Combine back: F(2)=1+0=1, F(3)=1+1=2, F(4)=2+1=3.

To find F(5), decompose into F(4) and F(3). F(4) decomposes into F(3) and F(2). F(3) decomposes into F(2) and F(1). F(2) decomposes into F(1) and F(0). Base cases: F(1)=1, F(0)=0. Combine: F(2)=1, F(3)=2, F(4)=3, F(5)=3+2=5.

ladders.title: Building Block — Function Calls with Return Values
ladders.desc: Master making function calls that return values and using those values in expressions.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves understanding how functions work in programming: you call a function, it executes, and returns a value that can be used in further computations. The intuition is that functions encapsulate logic, and their return values can be combined like any other data. For Fibonacci recursion, we need to call a function (itself) recursively, get return values from those calls, and add them. The algorithm practice is: define a function that returns an integer; inside, make calls to itself with modified parameters; use the return values in an arithmetic expression. Time and space are not primary here, but conceptually, each call takes time and stack space. Connection to L2: This enables the recursive decomposition by allowing functions to call themselves and combine results. Connection to L4: This relies on basic arithmetic operations. Common mistakes include not storing or using return values, misunderstanding recursion as iteration, or incorrect parameter passing. Real code pattern: def func(x): return something; result = func(a) + func(b).

examples.input: Call add(2,3) and multiply(4,5), then sum results
examples.trace: Define add(a,b): return a+b. Define multiply(a,b): return a*b. Call add(2,3) returns 5. Call multiply(4,5) returns 20. Sum: 5+20=26.

examples.input: Call square(3) and square(4), then sum results
examples.trace: Define square(x): return x*x. Call square(3) returns 9. Call square(4) returns 16. Sum: 9+16=25.

testCases.input: Call sum(1,2) where sum(a,b): return a+b
Call cube(2) where cube(x): return x*x*x

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Integer Addition and Conditional Checks
ladders.desc: Perform addition of integers and implement conditional statements for base cases.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on fundamental operations: adding two integers and using conditional statements (if-else) to control flow. The intuition is that computation often involves arithmetic and decisions based on values. For Fibonacci, we need to add results of recursive calls and check if n is 0 or 1 to stop recursion. The algorithm practice is: take two numbers, return their sum; check a condition, return different values based on it. Time and space are O(1) for these operations. Connection to L3: These operations are used within function calls to combine values and handle base cases. Connection to L5: This assumes knowledge of integers and booleans. Common mistakes include incorrect operator precedence, off-by-one in conditions, or missing else cases. Real code pattern: if condition: return value1; else: return value2; and result = a + b.

examples.input: Add 7 and 5, then check if sum > 10
examples.trace: Addition: 7+5=12. Conditional: 12 > 10 is True, so return True.

examples.input: Check if 3 is <= 1, else add 3 and 2
examples.trace: Conditional: 3 <= 1 is False, so go to else: add 3+2=5, return 5.

testCases.input: Check if 0 <= 1, return 'yes' if true, 'no' if false
Check if 2 <= 1, return 'yes' if true, 'no' if false
Add 4 and 6, then check if result == 10
Add 5 and 7, then check if result < 10
Check if 1 == 1, return 1 if true, 0 if false
Check if 1 == 0, return 1 if true, 0 if false
Check if 5 > 3, return 'greater' if true, 'less' if false

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Integers and Function Definition Syntax
ladders.desc: Understand integer data type and basic syntax for defining and calling functions.
ladders.explanation: This foundation level covers the simplest prerequisites: integers as a data type and the syntax for defining and calling functions in a programming language. The intuition is that programming builds on basic data and structures to express logic. Integers are whole numbers used for counting and arithmetic. Functions are reusable blocks of code that take inputs, perform operations, and return outputs. The algorithm concept is not complex here; it's about syntax: how to declare a function with parameters and return statement, and how to call it with arguments. Time and space are not applicable at this conceptual level. Connection to L4: This knowledge enables writing code that uses addition and conditionals within functions. Common mistakes include syntax errors in function definition, incorrect parameter types, or not understanding return values. Real code pattern: def function_name(parameter): return expression; and calling with function_name(argument).

examples.input: Define a function that returns the input integer, call with 5
examples.trace: Define: def identity(x): return x. Call: identity(5) returns 5.

examples.input: Define a function that returns double the input, call with 3
examples.trace: Define: def double(x): return 2*x. Call: double(3) returns 6.

testCases.input: Define function return_zero() that returns 0, call it
Define function return_one() that returns 1, call it
Define function add_one(x) that returns x+1, call with 4
Define function subtract_one(x) that returns x-1, call with 7
Define function is_positive(x) that returns True if x>0, call with 3
Define function is_positive(x) that returns True if x>0, call with -2
Define function square(x) that returns x*x, call with 2
Define function cube(x) that returns x*x*x, call with 3
Define function sum(a,b) that returns a+b, call with 2,3
Define function max(a,b) that returns a if a>=b else b, call with 5,3

approaches.name: Memoized Recursion O(n)
approaches.summary: Enhance naive recursion by storing previously computed Fibonacci values in a cache (e.g., an array or hash map) to avoid redundant calculations. This approach reduces time complexity to linear while maintaining the recursive structure, suitable for moderate N values where recursion is preferred.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Nth Fibonacci Number with Memoized Recursion
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of memoized recursion to compute the Nth Fibonacci number efficiently.
ladders.explanation:
The full problem requires implementing memoized recursion to compute the Nth Fibonacci number. The intuition is that naive recursion recalculates the same Fibonacci values repeatedly, leading to exponential time complexity. By storing computed values in a cache (memoization), we can avoid redundant calculations, reducing time complexity to O(n). The algorithm uses a recursive function that checks if fib(n) is already in the cache before computing it. If not, it computes fib(n) as fib(n-1) + fib(n-2) recursively, stores the result in the cache, and returns it. The base cases are fib(0)=0 and fib(1)=1. Time complexity is O(n) because each Fibonacci number from 0 to n is computed exactly once. Space complexity is O(n) for the recursion stack and cache. This connects to L1 by using the memoization helper function as the core subroutine. Common mistakes include forgetting to initialize the cache properly, not handling base cases correctly, or using global variables unsafely. Avoid these by using a local cache or passing it as a parameter, and ensuring base cases are defined before recursive calls.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Define a recursive function fib_memo(n, memo) where memo is a dictionary or array for caching.
2. If n is in memo, return memo[n].
3. If n == 0, return 0; if n == 1, return 1 (base cases).
4. Otherwise, compute result = fib_memo(n-1, memo) + fib_memo(n-2, memo).
5. Store result in memo[n] and return result.
6. Call fib_memo(n, {}) to compute the Nth Fibonacci number.

Real code pattern (Python-style pseudocode):
def fib_memo(n, memo):
    if n in memo:
        return memo[n]
    if n == 0:
        return 0
    if n == 1:
        return 1
    result = fib_memo(n-1, memo) + fib_memo(n-2, memo)
    memo[n] = result
    return result

def nth_fibonacci(n):
    return fib_memo(n, {})

examples.trace:
1. Call fib_memo(5, {}). memo is empty.
2. n=5 not in memo, not base case. Compute fib_memo(4, memo) + fib_memo(3, memo).
3. Call fib_memo(4, {}). n=4 not in memo. Compute fib_memo(3, memo) + fib_memo(2, memo).
4. Call fib_memo(3, {}). n=3 not in memo. Compute fib_memo(2, memo) + fib_memo(1, memo).
5. Call fib_memo(2, {}). n=2 not in memo. Compute fib_memo(1, memo) + fib_memo(0, memo).
6. Call fib_memo(1, {}). n=1 is base case, return 1. memo[1]=1.
7. Call fib_memo(0, {}). n=0 is base case, return 0. memo[0]=0.
8. fib_memo(2) computes 1+0=1, stores memo[2]=1, returns 1.
9. fib_memo(3) computes 1+1=2, stores memo[3]=2, returns 2.
10. fib_memo(4) computes 2+1=3, stores memo[4]=3, returns 3.
11. Back to fib_memo(5): need fib_memo(3, memo). memo[3]=2 from step 9.
12. fib_memo(5) computes 3+2=5, stores memo[5]=5, returns 5.

1. Call fib_memo(7, {}). memo empty.
2. n=7 not in memo. Compute fib_memo(6, memo) + fib_memo(5, memo).
3. Call fib_memo(6, {}). n=6 not in memo. Compute fib_memo(5, memo) + fib_memo(4, memo).
4. Call fib_memo(5, {}). From previous example, computes to 5, stores memo[5]=5.
5. Call fib_memo(4, {}). From previous example, computes to 3, stores memo[4]=3.
6. fib_memo(6) computes 5+3=8, stores memo[6]=8, returns 8.
7. Back to fib_memo(7): need fib_memo(5, memo). memo[5]=5 from step 4.
8. fib_memo(7) computes 8+5=13, stores memo[7]=13, returns 13.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Memoization Helper Function
ladders.desc: Implement the recursive function with memoization that computes Fibonacci numbers.
ladders.explanation:
This sub-routine is the core recursive function that uses memoization to compute Fibonacci numbers. The intuition is to avoid redundant calculations by caching results. The function takes an integer n and a cache (like a dictionary), and returns fib(n). It first checks if n is in the cache; if so, it returns the cached value immediately. Otherwise, it handles base cases (n=0 or n=1) or computes fib(n) recursively as fib(n-1) + fib(n-2), stores the result in the cache, and returns it. Time complexity for computing a single fib(n) with memoization is O(n) in the worst case, as each number up to n is computed once. Space complexity is O(n) for the cache and recursion stack. This connects to L0 by being the main function called in the full solution. Common mistakes include not passing the cache correctly between recursive calls or modifying a global cache improperly. Avoid these by passing the cache as a parameter or using a closure.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Define function fib_memo(n, memo).
2. If n is in memo, return memo[n].
3. If n == 0, set result = 0.
4. Else if n == 1, set result = 1.
5. Else, result = fib_memo(n-1, memo) + fib_memo(n-2, memo).
6. Store result in memo[n].
7. Return result.

Real code pattern (Python-style pseudocode):
def fib_memo(n, memo):
    if n in memo:
        return memo[n]
    if n == 0:
        result = 0
    elif n == 1:
        result = 1
    else:
        result = fib_memo(n-1, memo) + fib_memo(n-2, memo)
    memo[n] = result
    return result

examples.trace:
1. Call fib_memo(4, {}). memo empty.
2. n=4 not in memo, not base case. Compute fib_memo(3, memo) + fib_memo(2, memo).
3. Call fib_memo(3, {}). n=3 not in memo. Compute fib_memo(2, memo) + fib_memo(1, memo).
4. Call fib_memo(2, {}). n=2 not in memo. Compute fib_memo(1, memo) + fib_memo(0, memo).
5. Call fib_memo(1, {}). n=1 is base case, result=1, memo[1]=1, return 1.
6. Call fib_memo(0, {}). n=0 is base case, result=0, memo[0]=0, return 0.
7. fib_memo(2) computes 1+0=1, memo[2]=1, return 1.
8. fib_memo(3) computes 1+1=2, memo[3]=2, return 2.
9. Back to fib_memo(4): need fib_memo(2, memo). memo[2]=1 from step 7.
10. fib_memo(4) computes 2+1=3, memo[4]=3, return 3.

1. Call fib_memo(3, {0:0, 1:1}). memo has 0 and 1.
2. n=3 not in memo. Compute fib_memo(2, memo) + fib_memo(1, memo).
3. Call fib_memo(2, memo). n=2 not in memo. Compute fib_memo(1, memo) + fib_memo(0, memo).
4. fib_memo(1, memo): n=1 in memo, return memo[1]=1.
5. fib_memo(0, memo): n=0 in memo, return memo[0]=0.
6. fib_memo(2) computes 1+0=1, memo[2]=1, return 1.
7. Back to fib_memo(3): fib_memo(1, memo) returns memo[1]=1.
8. fib_memo(3) computes 1+1=2, memo[3]=2, return 2.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Memoization Technique for Recursion
ladders.desc: Apply memoization to store and reuse results in a recursive function.
ladders.explanation:
The core logic is the memoization technique itself: storing computed results in a cache to avoid redundant calculations in recursion. The intuition is that many recursive problems, like Fibonacci, have overlapping subproblems. By caching results, we transform an exponential-time recursion into a linear-time one. The technique involves maintaining a data structure (e.g., dictionary or array) that maps inputs to outputs. Before computing a result, check if it's already in the cache; if yes, return it immediately. Otherwise, compute it, store it in the cache, and then return it. Time complexity for accessing or storing in the cache is O(1) on average, making the overall algorithm efficient. Space complexity is O(n) for the cache. This connects to L1 by being the method used in the helper function. Common mistakes include using a cache that doesn't persist across recursive calls or not invalidating cache entries correctly. Avoid these by passing the cache as a parameter or using a persistent storage mechanism.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Initialize a cache (e.g., empty dictionary).
2. Define a recursive function f(x) that computes some value.
3. In f(x), first check if x is in cache. If so, return cache[x].
4. If not, compute result = ... (possibly using recursive calls to f).
5. Store result in cache[x].
6. Return result.

Real code pattern (Python-style pseudocode):
cache = {}
def f(x):
    if x in cache:
        return cache[x]
    # Compute result based on problem
    result = ...  # e.g., f(x-1) + f(x-2) for Fibonacci
    cache[x] = result
    return result

examples.input: Compute f(3) where f(0)=0, f(1)=1, f(n)=f(n-1)+f(n-2), cache={}
examples.trace:
1. Call f(3). cache empty.
2. x=3 not in cache. Compute f(2) + f(1).
3. Call f(2). x=2 not in cache. Compute f(1) + f(0).
4. Call f(1). x=1 not in cache. Base case: result=1. cache[1]=1, return 1.
5. Call f(0). x=0 not in cache. Base case: result=0. cache[0]=0, return 0.
6. f(2) computes 1+0=1. cache[2]=1, return 1.
7. Back to f(3): f(1) returns cache[1]=1.
8. f(3) computes 1+1=2. cache[3]=2, return 2.

examples.input: Compute f(4) where f(0)=0, f(1)=1, f(n)=f(n-1)+f(n-2), cache={0:0, 1:1}
examples.trace:
1. Call f(4). cache has 0 and 1.
2. x=4 not in cache. Compute f(3) + f(2).
3. Call f(3). x=3 not in cache. Compute f(2) + f(1).
4. Call f(2). x=2 not in cache. Compute f(1) + f(0).
5. f(1) returns cache[1]=1.
6. f(0) returns cache[0]=0.
7. f(2) computes 1+0=1. cache[2]=1, return 1.
8. f(3) computes 1+1=2. cache[3]=2, return 2.
9. Back to f(4): f(2) returns cache[2]=1.
10. f(4) computes 2+1=3. cache[4]=3, return 3.

ladders.title: Building Block — Recursive Function with Base Cases
ladders.desc: Implement a recursive function that computes Fibonacci numbers without memoization.

examples.trace:
1. Call fib_naive(4).
2. n=4 not base case. Compute fib_naive(3) + fib_naive(2).
3. Call fib_naive(3). Not base case. Compute fib_naive(2) + fib_naive(1).
4. Call fib_naive(2). Not base case. Compute fib_naive(1) + fib_naive(0).
5. Call fib_naive(1). Base case, return 1.
6. Call fib_naive(0). Base case, return 0.
7. fib_naive(2) returns 1+0=1.
8. Back to fib_naive(3): fib_naive(1) returns 1.
9. fib_naive(3) returns 1+1=2.
10. Back to fib_naive(4): need fib_naive(2). Recompute fib_naive(2) as in steps 4-7, returns 1.
11. fib_naive(4) returns 2+1=3.

1. Call fib_naive(3).
2. n=3 not base case. Compute fib_naive(2) + fib_naive(1).
3. Call fib_naive(2). Not base case. Compute fib_naive(1) + fib_naive(0).
4. fib_naive(1) returns 1.
5. fib_naive(0) returns 0.
6. fib_naive(2) returns 1+0=1.
7. Back to fib_naive(3): fib_naive(1) returns 1.
8. fib_naive(3) returns 1+1=2.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Function Calls and Return Values
ladders.desc: Understand how recursive functions call themselves and return values.
ladders.explanation:
This basic operation involves the mechanics of recursive function calls: a function that calls itself with modified parameters until it reaches a base case, then returns values back up the call stack. The intuition is that recursion breaks a problem into smaller subproblems of the same type. Each recursive call creates a new stack frame with its own local variables. When a base case is hit, it returns a value to the previous caller, which uses it to compute its result, and so on, until the original call returns the final result. Time and space complexity depend on the number of recursive calls; for Fibonacci naive recursion, it's exponential. This connects to L3 by being the mechanism used in the recursive function. Common mistakes include not having a base case (causing infinite recursion) or not returning values correctly. Avoid these by ensuring every recursive path leads to a base case and that return statements propagate values properly.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Define a recursive function f(x).
2. In f(x), check if x meets base condition. If yes, return base value.
3. Otherwise, compute result by calling f(x-1) (or similar).
4. Use the returned value to compute and return final result.

Real code pattern (Python-style pseudocode):
def f(x):
    if x == 0:  # base case
        return 1
    return x * f(x-1)  # recursive call, e.g., for factorial

examples.input: f(3) where f(0)=1, f(x)=x*f(x-1) for x>0
examples.trace:
1. Call f(3). x=3 not base case. Compute 3 * f(2).
2. Call f(2). x=2 not base case. Compute 2 * f(1).
3. Call f(1). x=1 not base case. Compute 1 * f(0).
4. Call f(0). x=0 is base case, return 1.
5. Back to f(1): computes 1 * 1 = 1, return 1.
6. Back to f(2): computes 2 * 1 = 2, return 2.
7. Back to f(3): computes 3 * 2 = 6, return 6.

1. Call f(2). x=2 not base case. Compute f(1) + f(0).
2. Call f(1). x=1 is base case, return 1.
3. Call f(0). x=0 is base case, return 0.
4. Back to f(2): computes 1 + 0 = 1, return 1.

testCases.input: f(1) where f(0)=1, f(x)=2*f(x-1) for x>0
f(2) where f(0)=1, f(x)=x+f(x-1) for x>0
f(3) where f(0)=0, f(x)=x+f(x-1) for x>0
f(4) where f(0)=1, f(x)=f(x-1)*2 for x>0
f(5) where f(0)=0, f(x)=x+f(x-1) for x>0
f(2) where f(0)=2, f(x)=f(x-1)+1 for x>0
f(3) where f(0)=3, f(x)=f(x-1)-1 for x>0
f(4) where f(0)=0, f(x)=f(x-1)+x for x>0

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Fibonacci Sequence Definition
ladders.desc: Understand the Fibonacci sequence: each number is the sum of the two preceding ones.
ladders.explanation:
This foundation concept is the definition of the Fibonacci sequence, a fundamental mathematical series. The intuition is that it models growth processes where each step depends on the previous two. The sequence starts with 0 and 1, and each subsequent number is the sum of the two before it: F(0)=0, F(1)=1, F(n)=F(n-1)+F(n-2) for n≥2. This recursive definition naturally leads to recursive algorithms. There is no time or space complexity here as it's just a definition. This connects to L4 by providing the rule for the recursive function calls. Common mistakes include misremembering the starting values or the recurrence relation. Avoid these by practicing with small examples.

Step-by-step to compute Fibonacci numbers manually:
1. Start with F(0)=0, F(1)=1.
2. For n=2: F(2)=F(1)+F(0)=1+0=1.
3. For n=3: F(3)=F(2)+F(1)=1+1=2.
4. Continue similarly for higher n.

Real code pattern (Python-style pseudocode for definition only):
# Fibonacci definition:
# F(0) = 0
# F(1) = 1
# F(n) = F(n-1) + F(n-2) for n >= 2

approaches.name: Iterative Dynamic Programming O(n)
approaches.summary: Solve the problem iteratively using a bottom-up dynamic programming approach by building the Fibonacci sequence from the base cases up to N, storing only the last two values to save space. This method is efficient and avoids recursion overhead, ideal for large N or environments with recursion limits.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Compute Nth Fibonacci Number Iteratively with O(1) Space
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of iterative dynamic programming to find the Nth Fibonacci number.
ladders.explanation:
This approach solves the Fibonacci sequence problem by building from the base cases upward, storing only the last two values to achieve O(1) space complexity. The intuition is that each Fibonacci number depends only on the two preceding numbers, so we can compute sequentially without storing the entire sequence. We start with the base cases: F(0)=0 and F(1)=1. For N≥2, we iterate from 2 to N, updating two variables representing F(i-1) and F(i-2). At each step, we compute F(i)=F(i-1)+F(i-2), then shift the variables forward. This avoids recursion overhead and stack overflow risks while maintaining O(n) time complexity. Common mistakes include incorrect base case handling (especially for N=0 or N=1), off-by-one errors in loop ranges, and forgetting to update both variables correctly. The algorithm connects to the parent problem by providing an efficient, space-optimized solution suitable for large N values.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. If N=0, return 0
2. If N=1, return 1
3. Initialize prev1=0 (F(0)), prev2=1 (F(1))
4. For i from 2 to N:
   a. current = prev1 + prev2
   b. prev1 = prev2
   c. prev2 = current
5. Return prev2

Time: O(n) - single loop through N values
Space: O(1) - only three variables used

Real code pattern:
def fibonacci(n):
    if n == 0: return 0
    if n == 1: return 1
    prev1, prev2 = 0, 1
    for i in range(2, n+1):
        current = prev1 + prev2
        prev1, prev2 = prev2, current
    return prev2

examples.trace:
N=5
Base cases: F(0)=0, F(1)=1
Initialize: prev1=0, prev2=1
Iteration i=2: current=0+1=1, update prev1=1, prev2=1
Iteration i=3: current=1+1=2, update prev1=1, prev2=2
Iteration i=4: current=1+2=3, update prev1=2, prev2=3
Iteration i=5: current=2+3=5, update prev1=3, prev2=5
Return prev2=5

N=8
Base cases: F(0)=0, F(1)=1
Initialize: prev1=0, prev2=1
i=2: current=0+1=1, prev1=1, prev2=1
i=3: current=1+1=2, prev1=1, prev2=2
i=4: current=1+2=3, prev1=2, prev2=3
i=5: current=2+3=5, prev1=3, prev2=5
i=6: current=3+5=8, prev1=5, prev2=8
i=7: current=5+8=13, prev1=8, prev2=13
i=8: current=8+13=21, prev1=13, prev2=21
Return 21

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Iterative Sequence Building with Two Variables
ladders.desc: Implement the core loop that builds Fibonacci sequence using only two variables.
ladders.explanation:
This sub-routine focuses on the iterative process of computing Fibonacci numbers while maintaining only the last two values. The intuition is that Fibonacci numbers have the recurrence relation F(n)=F(n-1)+F(n-2), meaning we only need the two previous numbers to compute the next one. By keeping two variables (representing F(i-2) and F(i-1)), we can compute F(i) and then update the variables for the next iteration. This is the heart of the space optimization - instead of storing all previous values in an array (which would be O(n) space), we use O(1) space. The algorithm starts after handling base cases, with prev1=0 (F(0)) and prev2=1 (F(1)). For each i from 2 to N, we compute current=prev1+prev2, then update prev1=prev2 and prev2=current. This effectively slides our two-variable window forward through the sequence. Common mistakes include incorrect initialization order, forgetting to update both variables, or using the wrong loop bounds. This sub-routine connects to L0 by providing the main computational engine that makes the full solution efficient.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Input: prev1, prev2 (initialized to F(0) and F(1)), N
2. For i from 2 to N:
   a. current = prev1 + prev2
   b. prev1 = prev2
   c. prev2 = current
3. Return prev2

Time: O(n) for the loop
Space: O(1) for the three variables

Real code pattern:
def iterative_build(prev1, prev2, n):
    for i in range(2, n+1):
        current = prev1 + prev2
        prev1, prev2 = prev2, current
    return prev2

examples.trace:
Input: prev1=0 (F(0)), prev2=1 (F(1)), n=4
i=2: current=0+1=1, update prev1=1, prev2=1
i=3: current=1+1=2, update prev1=1, prev2=2
i=4: current=1+2=3, update prev1=2, prev2=3
Return prev2=3 (which is F(4))

Input: prev1=1 (F(1)), prev2=1 (F(2)), n=5
i=3: current=1+1=2, update prev1=1, prev2=2
i=4: current=1+2=3, update prev1=2, prev2=3
i=5: current=2+3=5, update prev1=3, prev2=5
Return prev2=5 (which is F(5))

ladders.title: Core Logic — Fibonacci Recurrence with Minimal State
ladders.desc: Understand and implement the recurrence relation F(n)=F(n-1)+F(n-2) using only two variables.
ladders.explanation:
This level focuses on the mathematical insight that makes space optimization possible: the Fibonacci recurrence relation F(n)=F(n-1)+F(n-2) means each number depends only on the two immediately preceding numbers. Therefore, to compute F(n), we don't need to store the entire sequence up to n-1 - we only need F(n-1) and F(n-2). This is the core logic behind the iterative DP approach. We maintain two variables that represent these two most recent values, updating them as we progress through the sequence. The intuition is like walking through the sequence: at each step, you only need to remember where you just were and where you are now to determine where to go next. This is a form of state compression in dynamic programming. The algorithm involves initializing state for the first two numbers, then repeatedly applying the recurrence: new_value = current1 + current2, then shifting the window forward. Common mistakes include misunderstanding which variable represents which Fibonacci number, or trying to store more state than necessary. This connects to L1 by providing the mathematical foundation for why the two-variable approach works.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Initialize two variables: a=0 (F(0)), b=1 (F(1))
2. For computing F(n) where n≥2:
   a. Compute c = a + b
   b. Update: a = b, b = c
3. After n-1 iterations, b contains F(n)

Time: O(n) for n iterations
Space: O(1) for three variables

Real code pattern:
def recurrence_with_two_vars(n):
    a, b = 0, 1
    for _ in range(n):
        c = a + b
        a, b = b, c
    return a  # After n iterations, a is F(n)

examples.trace:
Compute F(4)
Initialize: a=0 (F(0)), b=1 (F(1))
Iteration 1: c=0+1=1, update a=1, b=1 (now a=F(1), b=F(2))
Iteration 2: c=1+1=2, update a=1, b=2 (now a=F(2), b=F(3))
Iteration 3: c=1+2=3, update a=2, b=3 (now a=F(3), b=F(4))
Iteration 4: c=2+3=5, update a=3, b=5 (now a=F(4), b=F(5))
Return a=3 (F(4))

Compute F(3)
Initialize: a=0, b=1
Iteration 1: c=0+1=1, a=1, b=1
Iteration 2: c=1+1=2, a=1, b=2
Iteration 3: c=1+2=3, a=2, b=3
Return a=2 (F(3))

ladders.title: Building Block — Variable Swapping in Iterative Computations
ladders.desc: Master the technique of updating multiple variables in a sequence computation.
ladders.explanation:
This building block focuses on the programming pattern of updating multiple variables in a specific order during iterative computations. In the Fibonacci algorithm, we need to update three variables: we compute a new value from two existing ones, then shift the old values to make room for the next computation. The key insight is that we must update variables in the correct order to avoid overwriting values we still need. The standard pattern is: compute new_value = a + b, then update a = b, and finally b = new_value. This ensures that a always holds F(i-2) and b holds F(i-1) for the next iteration. An alternative is simultaneous assignment: a, b = b, a+b. The intuition is that we're sliding a two-element window through the sequence: at each step, we drop the oldest value and add the newly computed one. This pattern appears in many iterative algorithms where each step depends on a fixed number of previous states. Common mistakes include updating variables in wrong order (like updating a before computing new value), or using temporary variables incorrectly. This connects to L2 by providing the mechanical implementation of the recurrence relation update step.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Initialize a=0, b=1
2. For each iteration:
   a. Compute sum = a + b
   b. Update a = b
   c. Update b = sum
3. After desired iterations, a contains the result

Time: O(1) per iteration
Space: O(1) for variables

Real code pattern:
def variable_swapping(n):
    a, b = 0, 1
    for i in range(n):
        # Method 1: Using temporary variable
        temp = a + b
        a = b
        b = temp
        
        # Method 2: Simultaneous assignment
        # a, b = b, a + b
    return a

examples.trace:
Initial: a=0, b=1
Iteration 1: sum=0+1=1, update a=1, b=1
Iteration 2: sum=1+1=2, update a=1, b=2
Iteration 3: sum=1+2=3, update a=2, b=3
Final: a=2, b=3

Initial: a=1, b=2
Iteration 1: sum=1+2=3, update a=2, b=3
Iteration 2: sum=2+3=5, update a=3, b=5
Final: a=3, b=5

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Integer Addition and Variable Assignment
ladders.desc: Perform basic arithmetic operations and variable updates in sequence.
ladders.explanation:
This level covers the fundamental operations needed for the Fibonacci algorithm: integer addition and variable assignment. The intuition is simple but crucial - we need to add two numbers together and store the result, then update variables to prepare for the next computation. In the context of Fibonacci, we repeatedly perform the operation: new_value = a + b, where a and b are integers. Then we update a and b for the next iteration. This requires understanding how variables store values and how assignment works (especially that assignment copies values, not creates references). The key insight is that operations happen in sequence: we must compute the sum before overwriting either a or b, since we need both original values for the addition. This is why temporary variables or simultaneous assignment are useful. Common mistakes include trying to update variables in the wrong order, misunderstanding operator precedence, or not using parentheses when needed. This connects to L3 by providing the atomic operations that make up the variable swapping pattern.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Read values of a and b
2. Compute sum = a + b
3. Store sum in a new variable or use it directly
4. Update a to hold b's value
5. Update b to hold the sum

Time: O(1) for each operation
Space: O(1) for variables

Real code pattern:
def basic_operations(a, b):
    # Step 1: Addition
    sum_result = a + b
    
    # Step 2: Variable updates
    # Method A: Using temporary variable
    temp = a
    a = b
    b = sum_result
    
    # Method B: Direct update (if order doesn't matter)
    # a, b = b, a + b
    
    return a, b, sum_result

examples.trace:
Input: a=3, b=5
Step 1: Compute sum = 3 + 5 = 8
Step 2: Update a = 5 (previous b value)
Step 3: Update b = 8 (the sum)
Result: a=5, b=8, sum=8

Input: a=0, b=1
Step 1: Compute sum = 0 + 1 = 1
Step 2: Update a = 1 (previous b value)
Step 3: Update b = 1 (the sum)
Result: a=1, b=1, sum=1

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Sequences and Iteration
ladders.desc: Learn to generate number sequences using loops and basic variables.
ladders.explanation:
This foundational level introduces the concept of generating numerical sequences through iteration. The Fibonacci sequence is a specific example of a sequence where each term is defined in terms of previous terms. The intuition is that we can create sequences by starting with initial values and repeatedly applying a rule to generate the next value. For Fibonacci, the rule is "add the last two numbers to get the next number." This requires understanding loops (repetition), variables (to store values), and basic arithmetic. We start with the first two numbers (0 and 1), then use a loop to generate subsequent numbers. At each iteration, we calculate the next number, update our stored values, and continue. This is one of the simplest examples of dynamic programming - building a solution incrementally from smaller subproblems. Common beginner mistakes include infinite loops, off-by-one errors in loop counts, and not initializing variables properly. This connects to L4 by providing the overall structure in which the basic operations are performed.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Initialize first two numbers of the sequence: a=0, b=1
2. Print or store these initial values
3. For i from 1 to n (where n is how many more numbers you want):
   a. Compute next = a + b
   b. Print or store 'next'
   c. Update: a = b, b = next
4. The sequence has been generated

Time: O(n) for generating n numbers
Space: O(1) if only printing, O(n) if storing all numbers

Real code pattern:
def generate_sequence(n_terms):
    a, b = 0, 1
    print(f"Term 1: {a}")
    if n_terms > 1:
        print(f"Term 2: {b}")
    
    for i in range(3, n_terms + 1):
        next_val = a + b
        print(f"Term {i}: {next_val}")
        a, b = b, next_val

examples.trace:
Initialize: a=0, b=1
Print: Term 1: 0
Print: Term 2: 1
Iteration 1 (i=3): next=0+1=1, print Term 3: 1, update a=1, b=1
Iteration 2 (i=4): next=1+1=2, print Term 4: 2, update a=1, b=2
Iteration 3 (i=5): next=1+2=3, print Term 5: 3, update a=2, b=3
Sequence: 0, 1, 1, 2, 3

Initialize: a=0, b=1
Print: Term 1: 0
Print: Term 2: 1
Iteration 1 (i=3): next=0+1=1, print Term 3: 1, update a=1, b=1
Sequence: 0, 1, 1
